item.title: AT&T Beats Estimates Buoyed by Internet, Phone Subscribers
item.description: AT&T Inc. reported revenue and profits in the first quarter that beat analysts’ estimates, as the No. 3 US wireless carrier continued to expand its fiber footprint and sell customers on its convergence strategy of subscribing to multiple products at once.

item.title: The Hottest Stocks Going Are Among the Cheapest, for Good Reason
item.description: Memory stocks sell at single-digit multiples on skepticism AI boom will last.

item.title: Russia Weighs Windfall Levy on Some Businesses Amid War Spending
item.description: Russia is considering a windfall tax on some commodity producers and banks as the Kremlin seeks additional funds to plug a budget gap amid rising war-related spending, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: MAHA’s Hopes for Healthier School Lunches Collide With Trump’s Spending Cuts
item.description: “We’re managing pennies.” Cafeterias are being pushed to serve healthier meals with less money.

item.title: Ferrexpo Slumps After Warning Cash May Run Out by End of August
item.description: Ferrexpo Plc slumped 13% after the Ukrainian iron ore producer warned that without fresh funding it could run out of cash by the end of August.

item.title: Germany Probably Eked Out Growth in Early 2026, Bundesbank Says
item.description: Germany’s economy, Europe’s largest, probably expanded slightly in the first quarter though was weighed down by the Iran war and longstanding structural issues, according to the Bundesbank.

item.title: Polymarket Loses Prediction-Market Lead After Delays, Blowback
item.description: Polymarket, the long-time leader in prediction market trading volume, has fallen behind its chief rival as it faces a growing list of operational stumbles in its attempt to reach a crucial audience: US customers.

item.title: OpenAI Poaches Airbnb Executive to be First Director for EMEA
item.description: OpenAI has hired its first managing director to oversee operations in Europe, helping guide its expansion in a key market where there are mounting political concerns about being overly reliant on US artificial intelligence services.

item.title: Naspers Mulls Options for South Africa’s Historic ‘City Press’
item.description: Naspers Ltd., Africa’s largest company by market value, is exploring strategic options for City Press, according to people familiar with the matter, a historic newspaper in the continent’s biggest economy.

item.title: Exxon Mobil Is Said to Consider Sale of Hong Kong Gas Stations
item.description: Exxon Mobil Corp. is exploring a sale of its gasoline station network in Hong Kong, according to people familiar with the matter, as the US oil and gas giant continues to trim its retail footprint globally.

item.title: Greece Announces Fresh Relief Measures After Budget Outperforms
item.description: Greece announced fresh economic relief measures worth around €500 million ($587.6 million), after data showed the country’s budget outperformed its targets last year.

item.title: Bank of England Deputy Governor Warns of Private Credit Crunch
item.description: Private credit risks a crunch akin to the squeeze suffered by the banking sector in the wake of the financial crisis, the Bank of England’s Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden said, while playing down the prospect of wider contagion.

item.title: US Customs Is Taking Tariff Refund Requests. Who’s Eligible?
item.description: The path to getting tariff refunds hit a major milestone on April 20 when US Customs and Border Protection began allowing companies to submit refund requests. But importers likely won’t see refunds for at least two to three months, according to the government.

item.title: EU Proposes Further Energy Measures to Mitigate Iran War Crisis
item.description: The European Union proposed measures such as optimizing jet fuel distribution, as it unveiled a plan to navigate the energy crisis caused by the Iran war.

item.title: AI Startup Has Helped Reverse Thousands of Denied Health Insurance Claims
item.description: Americans rarely fight back when insurers reject treatments their doctors have prescribed. Claimable is working to change that, with a little help from Mark Cuban.

item.title: Tesla’s Cooling AI Hype Makes Investors Yawn at Blowout Earnings
item.description: Tesla Inc. investors are in for a rare treat Wednesday afternoon: a blowout earnings report. The trouble is the actual numbers are likely to get overlooked as Wall Street seeks evidence that Elon Musk’s artificial intelligence and robotics ventures justify the stock’s sky-high valuation.

item.title: Australian Gas Exporter Santos Streamlines Business to Cut Costs
item.description: Santos Ltd. is restructuring the company’s oil and gas business in order to cut costs, according to an internal notice seen by Bloomberg, after a string of stymied takeover bids in recent years that have raised pressure to increase shareholder returns.

item.title: Russia Caps Fertilizer Exports Till December in Global Crunch
item.description: Russia extended fertilizer export quotas until December as a global deficit deepens due to the Iran war and disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, a key route for the seaborne trade in nutrients.

item.title: China Wind Turbine Maker Eyes Spain for Factory After UK Snub
item.description: Ming Yang Smart Energy Group Ltd. is considering building a wind turbine factory in Spain after the UK blocked the Chinese company’s plans for a facility in Scotland, citing risks to national security.

item.title: Deutsche Telekom, T-Mobile Would Be World’s Biggest M&A Deal
item.description: The technology, media and telecommunications sector has been behind the world’s largest public M&A deals and could be on course to deliver a fresh record.

item.title: Microsoft to Face UK Class Action Over Cloud Computing Licencing
item.description: Microsoft Corp. must face a UK class action trial over allegations it abused its dominant position in the market to overcharge businesses that used the Windows Server operating system and Azure, an antitrust tribunal has ruled.

item.title: Sale of Billionaire’s Row Mansion for £10 Million Sparks UK Suit
item.description: A property developer is suing a former tenant for around £4.5 million ($6.1 million), arguing she reneged on the purchase of a seven-bedroom mansion just off one of London’s most sought after streets.

item.title: South African Inflation Edges Up Before Iran Oil Shock Registers
item.description: South African annual inflation edged higher in March, even before the US-Israeli war on Iran triggered a surge in domestic fuel costs.

item.title: Telecom Megamerger Would Supercharge Transatlantic M&A Spree
item.description: Transatlantic mergers and acquisitions are booming, as companies block out the rising levels of geopolitical noise to chase their dream deals.

item.title: Daniel Yergin Sees a 'Different World' Emerging After the Hormuz Crisis
item.description: The acclaimed author on the aftermath of the war in Iran.

item.title: Nippon Life to Reduce Yen Bond Holdings Amid Iran Uncertainty
item.description: Nippon Life Insurance Co., Japan’s largest life insurer, plans to pare its holdings of yen-denominated bonds this fiscal year as it continues shifting from low-yielding debt securities to those with higher returns.

item.title: Odd Lots: Yergin on the Aftermath of the War in Iran (Podcast)
item.description: When it comes to the history of oil and energy, nobody is more famous or well respected than Daniel Yergin. He is the Vice Chairman of S&P Global, and the Pulitzer Prize winning author of both The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money, and Power and The New Map: Energy, Climate, and the Clash of Nations. So we had to get his insights on the war in Iran, and its historical significance. Yergin tells us that a “different world” will emerge from the crisis surrounding the closure of the Strait o

item.title: Unemployment Spikes for Key Chinese Age Group as AI Use Spreads
item.description: An unexpected deterioration in China’s labor market has reached the key demographic of early-career workers, as seasonal pressures amplified spillovers from the war in Iran while wider use of artificial intelligence raises risks for employment.

item.title: Norway Takes Over Planning for Rare Earth Mine From Municipality
item.description: Norway’s government will take over the planning from local authorities for what is touted to be Europe’s largest documented occurrence of rare earth elements, vital for the electronic and defense industries.

item.title: Indian Funds Buy 30-Year Bonds as Local Play Against Iran War
item.description: Some Indian fund managers are buying longer-term government bonds, saying these are less sensitive to global volatility driven by the Iran conflict.
